name: Briefing Note: Draft
description: Generates the full briefing note draft using the agreed structure, APS writing conventions, and explicit uncertainty flags.
prompt_template:
<role>
You are a senior APS policy analyst at the EL1 level, working in {{agency}}. You write clear, evidence-based policy advice in standard APS style: direct, impartial, and concise.
</role>

<task>
Continuing from the research and structure we've just developed in this conversation, now write the full {{length}} briefing note.

Topic: {{topic}}
Audience: {{audience}}
Purpose: {{purpose}}
{{#if key_documents}}
I'm also providing these reference documents to draw on:

<reference_documents>
{{key_documents}}
</reference_documents>
{{/if}}

Writing requirements:
- Begin with a clear PURPOSE line (one sentence)
- Use plain, direct language – avoid jargon unless it is standard for this audience
- Every paragraph should earn its place; cut anything that doesn't serve the reader
- Where specific statistics, dates, program details, or legislative references are needed but you are not certain they are accurate, write: [VERIFY: describe what needs checking]
- Where specific data should be inserted by the officer, write: [INSERT DATA: describe what's needed]
- End with a clear RECOMMENDATION or NEXT STEPS / ACTION REQUIRED section
- Do not fabricate specific figures, names, dates, or program details

This is a working draft for review – flag uncertainty explicitly rather than presenting uncertain content as fact.
</task>

variables.name: topic
variables.description: The policy topic for the briefing note

variables.name: audience
variables.description: Who will read this briefing note

variables.name: length
variables.description: Target length of the briefing note

variables.name: key_documents
variables.description: Externally available documents only – eg published annual reports, public policy documents, ABS/AIHW statistics, publicly available evaluations. Do not paste OFFICIAL: Sensitive or classified material into AI tools not approved for that classification. If your agency permits it for the AI tool you're using, you can also reference or upload internal documents directly in the tool rather than pasting them here.
variables.placeholder: Paste excerpts from available reference documents here – eg relevant sections of an annual report, published evaluation, or public statistics...

beginner.notes: Read the draft carefully before trusting it. Look for anything that sounds specific (statistics, names, dates, program amounts) – these need verification. The [VERIFY] and [INSERT DATA] flags are your action items.
beginner.prompt_modification: Add after 'This is a working draft for review': 'After the draft, list all [VERIFY] and [INSERT DATA] items in a numbered checklist to make them easy to work through.'

intermediate.notes: The draft is a starting point. Your domain expertise is the most valuable input at this stage – read critically and identify what feels wrong or missing before moving to quality gates.

advanced.notes: Consider providing the agreed structure from Step 2 as context here. If you have access to key documents or data, paste them in – the model produces significantly better drafts when given real reference material.

known_limitations: Only include information your agency permits you to enter into this tool. What is allowed depends on your AI tool, its data residency, and the classification of the material – check your agency's AI use policy.
Statistics and program details from the model's training data may be outdated or inaccurate – always verify.
The draft follows general APS conventions but may not match your agency's specific template or style guide.
The model may produce content that sounds authoritative but is fabricated. [VERIFY] flags are an indicator, not a guarantee.
